title: Merchandise exports by continent of destination
subtitle: Figures correspond to the value of [merchandise exports](#dod:merchandise-exports) by continental destination as a share of [gross domestic product](#dod:gdp) (GDP). All partner countries are classified into continent groupings according to OWID's classification.
caption:
Merchandise exports by continent of destination, India, 1949 to 2014

Figures correspond to the value of merchandise exports by continental destination as a share of gross domestic product (GDP). All partner countries are classified into continent groupings according to OWID's classification.

This stacked area chart tracks the value of India's merchandise exports as a percentage of its gross domestic product from 1949 to 2014, categorized by the continent of destination. The vertical axis measures the share of GDP, ranging from 0% to 18%, while the horizontal axis spans the years. The data shows a distinct U-shaped trajectory over the 65-year period. In 1949, total exports stood at approximately 6% of GDP, then declined steadily to a low point of roughly 3% in the late 1960s. From the 1970s onward, the aggregate share began a sustained rise, accelerating significantly after 1990 and reaching a peak of over 18% around 2008 before settling near 16% in 2014. The composition of trade shifted visibly across the decades. Exports to Asia, represented by the blue area at the base, expanded from under 2% in 1949 to become the dominant component, accounting for roughly 8% of GDP by 2014. Exports to Western Europe, shown in maroon, also grew substantially, rising from a minimal share to roughly 3% by the end of the period. The green band representing North America shows moderate growth, while exports to South America, Africa, Eastern Europe, and Oceania remain as thin layers at the top of the stack, indicating smaller shares throughout the timeline. The estimates come from dyadic transactions data, where series like Western Europe correspond to the sum of exports between all Western European countries divided by the GDP of Western Europe. The most prominent visible feature is the sharp divergence between the pre-1970 contraction and the post-1990 expansion in export intensity relative to the size of the economy.
